observations.managementAction:
The Inventory Committee is yet to schedule the conduct of physical count of the PPE of the Municipality. Subsequently, once the RPCPPE is prepared by the Inventory Committee, it will be reconciled with the accounting records.                                


The GSO and MAO are exerting their efforts in retrieving documents to prepare PPELC.
                 


GSO is now rigorously implementing the PAR over the PPE being given to Municipal Officials and Employees.
observations.reason:
Reiterated in Part 2, Finding No. 1, page 31

The Inventory Committee has not yet conducted the required physical count of all properties of the Municipality. Consequently the RPCPPE has not been prepared.

observations.managementAction:
The Municipal Mayor already created an Inventory Committee to conduct the physical count of the PPE of the Municipality and to prepare the physical inventory reports.

The Inventory Committee has not yet scheduled a physical count of the Municipality’s Property, Plant, and Equipment (PPE), including the Land Account. Once completed, the RPCPPE will be prepared by the Inventory Committee and subsequently reconciled with accounting records.


Due to budget constraints this year, the Municipal Government has been unable to initiate the transfer of Land ownership under its name.
observations.reason:
Reiterated in Part 2, Finding No. 3, page 43

The reconciliation between the records of the Municipal Accountant and the GSO is currently ongoing. During the CY 2024 audit, discrepancies were noted when compared with the Municipal Assessor’s records, primarily due to the lack of coordination and reconciliation with the said office.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to direct the Municipal Treasurer to deposit all collections regularly and intact within the prescribed period to the authorized government depository bank as required in Section 69 of P.D 1445 and other existing regulations to avoid possible theft/ loss or misuse.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Treasurer ensures that collections are deposited daily or the next banking day.
observations.reason:
Reiterated in Part 2, Finding No. 9, page 71

An examination of the Municipality's Summary of Collections for the period from June to September 2024 revealed that the Treasury Office did not consistently adhere to the established procedure of depositing daily collections in full and on the day of receipt. 

Further, actual remittances are sometimes delayed, primarily due to the practice of retaining a portion of collections as a “change fund” for use in transactions on the next business day.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to direct the GAD Focal Person to carefully identify and assess PPAs utilizing the methodologies outlined in the PWC-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC 2016-01 to ensure that gender-related issues and concerns are effectively addressed and that procedures are done accordingly for proper implementation and monitoring of LGU GAD Plan and Budget.
observations.managementAction: PPAs for Gender and Development Plan were carefully identified to address the gender related issues and concerns
observations.reason:
Reiterated in Part 2, Finding No. 13, page 84

Our review noted that some GAD PPAs do not fully address gender issues or promote gender equality and women’s empowerment.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended the Municipal Mayor through its Local Sanggunian and other officials concerned to expedite the drafting of the amended Municipal Local Revenue Code subject to approval by the SP of the Province of Pangasinan to cope with the dynamic and everchanging economic atmosphere and business environment.
observations.managementAction: The revised Local Revenue Code was already transmitted to the Sangguniang Panlalawigan, for approval/ review.
observations.reason: The Municipality of Umingan resubmitted its Revised Revenue Code to the Sangguniang Panlalawigan of Pangasinan for review, deliberation, and final approval.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our previous recommendations that the Municipal Mayor direct the MPDC to coordinate with the concerned departments, such as the Municipal Assessor Office, Municipal Social Welfare Department, Office of the Municipal Agriculturist, Municipal Health Officer, members of the SB, among others, to aid in the database formulation for expeditious approval of the plans/programs necessary in the drafting of an updated CLUP.

We also have recommended that the MPDC convene with the Local Development Council to identify the bottlenecks that might have contributed to the delay of the approval of the CLUP and to provide viable solutions in resolving any identified constraints.
observations.managementAction: Budget constraint for the current year.
observations.reason:
Reiterated in Part 2, Finding No. 16, page 100

According to an inquiry with the MPDC, the Municipal Government is currently engaged in data gathering activities, which include an inventory of roads and bridges, identification of flood-prone areas, and the creation of various maps, such as soil maps, slope maps, geological maps, fault line maps, and flood maps for the entire jurisdiction of the Municipality of Umingan.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Engineer the following:

For similar future projects, conduct effective and efficient planning through project site inspection by technical personnel to avoid delay and adjustments in the plan and implementation of project; and

Regular monitoring in the implementation of the ongoing and succeeding projects to ensure completion thereof within the target completion dates to achieve utmost benefits for the public.

In view of the foregoing, we recommend that the management provide the Audit Team of copies of approved time extensions, suspensions and variation orders or other related documents that will prove that the projects are not delayed, otherwise impose liquidated damages against the claims of the contractors who have incurred delay in the implementation of infrastructure projects or apply any other action as prescribe in RA 9184.
observations.reason:
Reiterated in Part 2, Finding No. 15, page 96

In our audit of CY 2024, we noted that out of the four of the delayed projects in CY 2023, only the Rehabilitation of Public Plaza Phase 3 was completed during the 1st Quarter of CY 2025.

Further, there were no available updates on the completion status of the remaining three projects.

In addition, two more delayed projects were added during the CY 2024.

The Audit Team has not received copies of approved time extensions, suspensions, variation orders, or any related documents.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
Instruct the Inventory Committee to prioritize conducting a thorough and complete physical count of all properties at least once a year. Subsequently, they should prepare the RPCPPE in the proper format and submit a copy of the reports to the Audit Team by January 31 each year. Additionally, adopt a phased approach for the physical count and record reconstruction, starting with current year acquisitions and progressively addressing previous years, especially where full physical verification may be challenging. This incremental strategy will demonstrate an ongoing commitment and make the task more manageable.
Instruct the Municipal GSO to maintain Property Cards for all the PPEs of the Municipality. Proper maintenance of these records is crucial for accurate tracking and accountability of physical assets.
Require the Municipal Accountant to maintain PPELC and reconcile it with the PCs of the Municipal GSO. This collaborative effort is essential to verify the accuracy of recorded balances and to identify and resolve discrepancies promptly.
Direct the Property Officer, the Inventory Committee, and the Municipal Accountant to adhere to the guidelines and procedures set forth under COA Circular 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020, to reflect more reliable balances of PPE accounts.

We have reiterated our prior recommendation that the Municipal Mayor direct:
The Municipal Accountant, Municipal GSO and Municipal Assessor to coordinate and reconcile their respective records; and
The Municipal Assessor and Municipal Accountant to ensure the proper valuation of the Land account for fairly presented financial statements. In cases where valuation data or supporting documents are unavailable, the Municipality may refer to Section 6.2.12 of COA Circular No. 2020-006 for guidance on alternative valuation methods
Further, require the following:
The Municipal Accountant to maintain Real Property Ledger Cards for all acquired Land of the Municipal Government; and
The Municipal GSO to maintain Property Cards per category of property, plant and equipment for all properties received and under his accountability to be reconciled regularly with the accounting records and the corresponding RPLCs maintained by the Municipal Accountant.

We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
Instruct the Municipal Engineer, in coordination with the Municipal Accountant and GSO, to provide the complete description and cost segregation of road components for road projects.
Direct the Municipal Accountant to:
Maintain the complete Local Road Network Ledger Card (LRNLC) along with its subsidiary records for each road network, and regularly reconcile it with the records kept by the GSO and the Inventory Committee.
Prepare a journal entry voucher to take up the adjusting journal entries due to the improper application of residual value to correct the understatement of the Depreciation Expenses and Accumulated Depreciation for the previous and current year, once the proper segregation of depreciable components has been completed.
Reassess previously recognized impairment losses, and in the absence of sufficient and appropriate documentary evidence, the Municipality may derecognize such impairments.
Provide additional disclosure regarding the total Road Network account in the Notes to Financial Statements in compliance with Item VI.2 of COA Circular No. 2015-008.
Require the Municipal GSO to:
To render the Report on Local Road Network (RLRN) at the end of the accounting period.
Maintain Local Road Network Property Card (LRNPC) for all roads and its components and regularly reconcile it with the balance per Local Road Network Ledger Card.
In coordination with the Municipal Engineer, to maintain a Local Road Inventory and Road Map.
Require the Inventory Committee to conduct the annual Physical Inventory and submit the Report on the Physical Count of Local Road Network (RPCLRN) to both the Audit Team and the Accounting Office not later than January 31 of each year. Also, invite a representative from the COA to act as a witness in the physical count.

We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
Instruct the Municipal Accountant to:
Make necessary adjustments for the misclassified account at year-end, as follows:
To reclassify issued semi-expendable properties in CY 2024
Record the PPE items below the capitalization threshold as inventory under the appropriate semi-expendable property account upon purchase, and shall be recognized as semi-expendable expense upon issuance to the end-user, following COA Circular No. 2022-004 and COA Circular No. 2024-006.
Refer to Annex C of COA Circular No. 2024-006 as a guide in preparing adjustments for prior-year acquisitions of property and equipment below the ₱50,000 capitalization threshold that were improperly charged to PPE or other inventory accounts, if any.
To enhance accountability and internal control over semi-expendable property, the Municipal Government to ensure the proper preparation and maintenance of the following prescribed forms, registries, and reports, as applicable, under Item 4.7 of COA Circular No. 2022-004 dated May 31, 2022:
Semi-Expendable Property Card (SPC)
Semi-Expendable Property Ledger Card (SPLC)
Registry of Semi-Expendable Property Issued (RegSPI)
Inventory Transfer Report (ITR)
Receipt of Returned Semi-Expendable Property (RRSP)
Report of Semi-Expendable Property Issued (RSPI)
Report on the Physical Count of Semi-Expendable Property (RPCSP)
Report of Lost, Stolen, Damaged, or Destroyed Semi-Expendable Property (RLSDDSP)
Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Semi-Expendable Property (IIRUSP)

We have recommended the Municipal Mayor to direct the following:
The Municipal Accountant to make necessary adjustments for unreleased checks at year-end, as follows:
Cash in Bank - Local Currency, Current Account	₱2,610,093.38
Appropriate Payable Accounts			₱2,610,093.38
To revert unreleased checks as of December 31, 2024
The Municipal Treasurer to prepare and furnish the Municipal Accountant with a list of unreleased checks at the end of the year. Further, proper coordination between the Accounting and Treasurer’s Office should be made to monitor these unreleased checks so that appropriate adjustments can be made in a timely manner.

We recommend that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to:
Prepare an adjusting journal entry to recognize the accrual of interest expense to ensure compliance with IPSAS requirements and enhance the reliability of the financial statements, as follows:
Interest Expense/Prior Year Adjustments	₱508,548.95
Interest Payable				₱508,548.95
To recognize the accrual of interest expenses on loans for CY 2024
Review and verify the updated amortization schedule, and complete the reconciliation between the Municipality's loan records and the bank's updated amortization schedules. This will ensure the accurate classification of the current and non-current portions of the loans, thereby preventing future discrepancies. Once the reconciliation is finalized and verified, promptly reclassify the Loans Payable – Domestic in both the Statement of Financial Position and the Notes to the Financial Statements to reflect the correct classification.

For the Municipality to have the assurance that inventories are safeguarded against the risk of misapplication or misuse, we recommend that the Municipal Mayor direct the following:
The Municipal Accountant to record inventory using the prescribed perpetual method of accounting on all purchased supplies and materials, except those purchased through PCF.
The concerned offices shall maintain monitoring reports of all receipt and issuances of inventories held for stocks or distributions to the public. These reports shall be submitted periodically to the Municipal Accountant to serve as supporting documents for the timely and appropriate adjustment of inventory balances to the corresponding expense accounts.
The General Service Officer and Municipal Accountant to:
Prepare and maintain complete SCs and SLCs for each type of supplies and materials, respectively; and
Conduct regular reconciliation of the ledger and the stock cards to ensure that all purchases and issuances are properly accounted for.
The Inventory Committee to conduct a comprehensive physical count of all types of inventory items semestrally, prepare the RPCI and submit the same to the Auditor concerned not later than July 31 and January 31 of each year for the first and second semesters, respectively. Also, invite a representative from the COA to act as a witness in the physical count.

We have recommended the Municipal Mayor to instruct the Municipal Treasurer to:
Enforce the preparation of the daily RCD by each collecting officer at the close of every business day as prescribed by the Manual on NGAS for LGUs, as this will serve as a critical document for tracking and verifying all collections received and remitted to the LO. Moreover, the RCD should be signed by the collecting officer to confirm the accuracy of the collections, and by the LO or Municipal Treasurer to acknowledge receipt; and
Discontinue the practice of withholding some of the collections for use as a "change fund" for the following business day. Instead, ensure that all collections are promptly remitted to the LO and utilize the available cash on hand for any necessary change transactions.

To protect the interest of the Municipal Government from any adverse claim by third parties over the government properties, we recommend that the Municipal Mayor to:
Require the OIC-Municipal Budget Officer to allocate funds to cover all expenses associated with the land registration process;
Instruct the Municipal Assessor, in coordination with the Municipal GSO, to prioritize the application for transfer and registration of ownership for the twenty-one parcels of land; and
Direct the Municipal GSO to exhaust all its efforts to complete its files and records of the Certificate of titles and other proof of ownership.

With the foregoing observations, we have recommended the Municipal Mayor to direct the Municipal Treasurer to:
Ensure that all collectors responsible for accountable forms or collections comply with the fidelity bonding requirements as outlined in TC No. 02-2019, dated April 25, 2019. The fidelity bond acts as a safeguard for the agency, providing security for Accountable Officers in cases of defalcations, shortages, or unrelieved accountabilities, covering actual losses up to the specified bond amount.
Strict adherence to the requirements specified in Sections 5.1 and 5.3 of TC No. 02-2019 specifically the appropriate bond premium category must be selected to fully cover the maximum cash accountabilities of each officer, as determined by prior transactions. This action will help protect the Municipal Government’s interests by ensuring sufficient coverage against the loss or misapplication of public funds.

We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to refrain from delaying the release of the shares of the barangay from RPT collections. These shares should be remitted to the Barangays on a quarterly basis within five days after the end of each quarter in accordance with the provision of Section 271 (d) of RA No. 7160 to enable the barangays to use the additional income for their operation and PPAs, which are beneficial to their constituents; and
Municipal Accountant to prepare a monthly schedule showing the computation and the amount of RPT payable to each barangay and record the same in individual subsidiary ledgers to facilitate the timely and quarterly release of RPT shares of the barangays.

We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor the following:
Initiate the reconstitution of the GFPS in accordance with Section 4.1.A.2 of PCW-DBM-DILG-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01 for a comprehensive identification and response to GAD issues and concerns within the Municipality.
Direct the GAD Focal Person to:
In coordination with the MPDC, expedite the completion of the Community-Based Monitoring System (CBMS) and maintain an updated GAD Database to facilitate the identification of gender issues in the GAD Plan and Budget.
In coordination with other departments/offices, carefully evaluate the suggested PPAs to ensure they effectively address gender-related issues and refrain from charging expenses to the GAD Fund that are not related in addressing gender-related issues. The municipality may also attribute part or entirely their GAD PPAs, provided it must undergo proper assessment using the HGDG to determine their gender-responsiveness and alignment with the objectives of gender mainstreaming.

We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:
Municipal Treasurer to prepare and furnish the Municipal Accountant with a duly certified list of taxpayers, showing their names and the amount due and collectible based on the Real Property Tax Account Register/Taxpayer’s index card to establish an accurate and reliable basis for setting up of receivables for RPT and SET at the beginning of the year, ensuring the fair presentation of the accounts in the financial statements.
Municipal Accountant to discontinue the practice of closing RPT and SET receivable balances at year-end to accurately present the actual amounts due from the taxpayers in the financial statements.
Conduct a sustained information campaign and tax initiative to raise constituency awareness regarding the advance payment of real property taxes and to access discounts, encouraging prompt payment and enhancing the Municipality's collection efficiency for RPT/SET.
Continue sending letters to delinquent taxpayers and post notices of delinquency at the main entrance of the municipal hall and in a publicly accessible and conspicuous location in each Barangay of the local government unit concerned. Additionally, the Municipality may utilize available legal remedies to generate more revenue that can be allocated for the implementation of additional programs and projects for the benefit of its constituents.

We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Engineer the following:
For similar future projects, conduct effective and efficient planning through project site inspection by technical personnel to avoid delay and adjustments in the plan and implementation of project.
Regular monitoring in the implementation of the ongoing and succeeding projects to ensure completion thereof within the target completion dates to achieve utmost benefits for the public.
Suspend the work, when necessary, in a timely manner through a written order duly received by the contractor in accordance with Section 10 of Annex “E” of 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184. Accordingly, indicate the suspension order and adjust the contract timeline in the Quarterly Report on PPAs.
To furnish the Audit Team with copies of approved time extensions, suspension orders, variation orders, or any related documents that would justify and validate that the subject projects are not delayed. In the absence of such documentation, impose the appropriate liquidated damages against the contractors responsible for the delays in the implementation of infrastructure projects. If payment cannot be collected, deduct the corresponding amount from the contractor’s final billing or pursue other applicable remedies as provided under RA 9184.

We reiterated our previous recommendations that the Municipal Mayor direct the MPDC to:
Coordinate with the concerned departments, such as the Municipal Assessor Office, Municipal Social Welfare Department, Office of the Municipal Agriculturist, Municipal Health Officer, members of the SB, among others, to aid in the database formulation for expeditious approval of the plans/programs necessary in the drafting of an updated CLUP;
Convene with the Local Development Council to identify the bottlenecks that might have contributed to the delay of the approval of the CLUP and to provide viable solutions in resolving any identified constraints.
Furthermore, to address the continued delays and expedite the completion of the CLUP, we recommend the following actions
Provide additional technical and support personnel to the Municipal Planning and Development Office to assist in the data gathering, mapping, and preparation processes required for the CLUP formulation; and
Require the Municipal Budget Officer to allocate sufficient funds to support all activities critical to the completion of the CLUP, including data collection, map development, and stakeholder consultations.

We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the BAC the following:
Through the Bids and Awards Committee, to require the end-user units to prepare their respective PPMPs in the prescribed format and properly accomplished for their different PAPs and submit to the Budget Office for evaluation in order to ensure consistency with the Procuring Entity’s ensuing year budget proposal.  The PPMPs shall be forwarded to the BAC Secretariat for consolidation into an indicative APP and to the BAC for final recommendation of the appropriate procurement modality.  The APP shall then be submitted to the GPPB as provided in Section 7.3.5 of the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184.
Ensure that complete and duly approved Standard Philippine Bidding Documents are prepared and issued to all prospective bidders. These documents should also be attached to the corresponding contracts to facilitate proper review and verification.
Strictly adhere to the provisions of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA 9184 and DPWH D.O.  No. 197, Series of 2016, by ensuring that the ABC is prepared using the prescribed standard form. The ABC should be attached to all contracts as part of the Detailed Engineering Documents to facilitate proper verification, and promote transparency in the procurement process.
Through the BAC Secretariat, to strictly adhere to and document all mandatory procurement activities, particularly the execution of Pre-Procurement, in accordance with the Section 20.2 of 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184.
Through the BAC Secretariat, to strictly comply with Section 25.7 of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 by ensuring the timely issuance of written notification letters to all bidders who submitted their bid proposals, regardless of the number of participating bidders.
Through the BAC Secretariat, to ensure that the certifications regarding the advertisement of the ITB and the posting of the NOA and NTP in a conspicuous location on the Municipality's premises are properly prepared. These certifications must also specify the exact locations and dates of posting and be supported by time-stamped photographs or other verifiable evidence. These should be attached to the procurement documents to validate compliance with posting requirements.
Through its BAC Secretariat, to maintain a comprehensive and verifiable record-keeping system, such as a logbook, to document the issuance of Bid Documents. This record should clearly indicate the date of issuance, official receipt number, name of the recipient or bidder, project name, and other relevant details for each transaction. Copies of these records should be attached to the corresponding contracts to ensure transparency and facilitate verification
To strictly require the winning bidder to submit the DOLE-approved CSHP prior to contract signing for all infrastructure projects. Furthermore, it is recommended that the BAC advise and encourage contractors to promptly begin the preparation and submission of the CSHP to the appropriate DOLE Regional or Field Office upon receipt of the NOA, in order to secure early approval and avoid delays in the commencement of construction activities.
Require the submission of the construction methods, and PERT/CPM or other acceptable project scheduling tools by the winning bidder prior to contract signing, in strict compliance with Section 37.2.3(f) of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184
Through the BAC Secretariat, to strictly comply with the prescribed timelines for posting the NOA, NTP and the Approved Contract on the PhilGEPS website. Moreover, the BAC Secretariat should ensure that all notices clearly indicate the date of receipt by the concerned bidder to establish and verify the actual dates of issuance and compliance with regulatory timelines.
Adopt the latest standard forms and templates issued by the GPPB, including the prescribed Contract Agreement format under GPPB Circular No. 04-2020.

In view of the foregoing, we have recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

Direct the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Engineer to reconcile their records on CIP, at least quarterly, to facilitate the updated status of ongoing PPAs of the Municipality which will eventually serve as the basis for recognizing accruals in the books of accounts; and

Direct the Municipal Accountant to make necessary journal entries to recognize the accrued cost on the actual work accomplishments of the foregoing projects as of year-end. The proposed adjusting journal entries is as follows:

CIP – (Specific Asset Account) 	xxx
			Accounts Payable		xxx

For the Municipal Accountant to recognize Advances to Contractors in the event of payment of a mobilization fee, rather than through the CIP account.
observations.managementAction: Adjustments was already made in the books of accounts.
observations.reason:
Our review of CIP accounts and their ongoing status of the PPAs showed that the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Engineer have reconciled their records as of December 31, 2024.
While a minor variance was noted, attributed to rounding off the percentage of completion, this discrepancy is considered immaterial and does not affect the overall reliability of the records.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the following:

The Municipal Budget Officer and Municipal Accountant to (i) comply with the provisions of IPSAS 24 on the preparation of SCBAA; and (ii) reconcile their records so that any resulting discrepancies can be immediately verified and corrected; and

The Municipal Accountant to provide disclosures on the face of the SCBAA and/or in the Notes to FS the (i) changes between the original and final budgets; (ii) material discrepancies between the budget and actual amounts; and (iii) reconciliation of actual amounts per SCBAA and per financial statements, to enhance the transparency of the Municipality’s financial statements.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant provides adequate disclosures on the identifies reconciling items of SCBAA.
observations.reason: The Reconciliation Statement was prepared by the Municipal Accountant.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended the Municipal Mayor to instruct the Municipal Accountant to provide the lacking disclosures in the Notes to Financial Statement using the prescribed format as provided by COA Circular 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012 and comply with the disclosure requirements of IPSAS and MNGAS for LGUs. Conformity with pertinent accounting guidelines from other relevant rules and regulations should also be practiced by adequately inserting a concise narrative and detailed schedule of material balances to ensure that relevant information is available to the intended users of the Financial Statements.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant provides adequate disclosures in the Notes to Financial Statement using the prescribed formats.
observations.reason: The prescribed format of disclosure has been incorporated into the Notes to FS.

observations.recommendation:

observations.managementAction: Adjustments were already made in the books of accounts.
observations.reason: The Municipal Accountant made adjusting entry through JEV No. 1002024-3920A to effect the audit recommendation.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant and the HRMO to establish control mechanisms preventing employees from availing of loans that will eventually reduce their monthly net take home pay below Five Thousand Pesos (₱5,000.00) as provided in the GAA FY 2023 so that adequate funds are available for the basic needs of each employee.
observations.managementAction: HRMO refrains from approving loans of employees that reduce their net take-home pay to below P5,000.00.
observations.reason: According to the HRMO, no additional loans were approved at the start of the 2nd semester of 2024. As a result of this control, the number of affected employees significantly decreased to only two.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended the Municipal Mayor to instruct the Municipal Treasurer the following:
 
To thoroughly examine the replenishment of the PCF and to avoid granting cash advances to the PCF Custodian in excess of the required amount for replenishment, which could lead to the accumulation of a significant balance of PCF in their possession; and

To carefully manage its resources especially the PCF since this can be a potent source of mishandling and abuse. Strict compliance over PCF policy and COA Circular 97-002.
observations.managementAction: Review of the records disclosed that replenishments made were not equal to the total amount of expenditures incurred hence, defeating the purpose of an imprest fund system.
observations.reason: Review of the PCF Custodian's cashbook for August 2024 revealed no significant accumulated balance at month-end. A sample review for August 2024 showed that replenishments were made only twice, with a PCF balance of P2,498.11 remaining at the end of the month.

observations.recommendation:
We have reiterated prior year’s audit recommendations that the Municipal Mayor instruct the following:

The BAC to deliberate properly on the appropriate mode of procurement to be used, before the Municipality acquires the needed goods, thus avoiding a similar scenario to happen; and

To stop the practice of splitting purchase requisitions or contracts and other related transactions pursuant to COA Circular No. 76-41, to promote effective competition and transparency in the procurement process and obtain a fair and equitable contract for the best interest of the government.
observations.managementAction: The implementation of projects, programs, and activities under the LDRRM Fund were done thru competitive public bidding
observations.reason: Verification of available records disclosed that procurement activities were done thru competitive bidding or applicable alternative mode, as applicable.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor enjoin the Municipal Engineer to consider the application of geo-tagging of projects due to its effectiveness in providing a more transparent, accurate and reliable report of project accomplishments and further as a deterrent to possible duplication and overlapping of infrastructure projects.
observations.managementAction: Municipal Engineering Office started the application of geo-tagging of projects to provide an accurate and reliable report of project accomplishments.
observations.reason:
Verification of the implementation of geo-tagging was conducted through a sampling of infrastructure projects implemented by the Municipality.

Projects sampled, including the slope protection project, rehabilitation of the box culvert, and the multi-purpose canteen, were found to be supported by geo-tagged photographs.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor through its MDRRMO finalize the formulation of the PSCP to strengthen the risk management capabilities of the Municipal Government and enable it to manage its vulnerabilities in the event of disruptive events then submit the same to the Municipal Mayor for approval, and to the SB for the passage of a resolution to institutionalize the PSCP.

We further recommended that LDRRM Officer, in the event the PSCP has been approved, adopt a monitoring of operational requirements like the submission of PSCP and continuous update of the plan depending on the availability of other useful information
observations.managementAction: PSCP was already approved by the SB
observations.reason:
Approved by SB through SB Resolution No. 400, S-2024.

The LDRRM Officer submitted the PSCP of the Municipality to the Policy Development and Planning Section of Region I, which was subsequently forwarded to the Office of Civil Defense – Regional Office I.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended the Municipal Mayor to:

Enjoin the Municipal Accountant who certified the completeness of the supporting documents, together with the officers or employees who inspected and accepted the delivery to submit delivery receipt and any well documented proof of actual delivery, as well as proof of acceptance by the beneficiaries of the livelihood project; and

Direct the GSO, Property and Supply Custodian, and Inspectorate Committee to ensure that the Inspection and Acceptance Report (IAR) accurately reflects the actual quantity of items delivered.
observations.managementAction: Retrieval of documents relative to this project is still ongoing.
observations.reason: A review of the IARs for various procurements disclosed that the quantity of items delivered was reflected, and both the inspection and acceptance portions of the reports were properly accomplished.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to instruct Municipal Accountant to ensure that additional disclosures were included as to the total cash payments for acquisition of long-term assets and that the effects of transactions from Statement of Financial Position to Statement of Cash Flows and even to Statement of Comparison of Budget and Actual Amounts are presented in a comparable and understandable manner.
observations.reason: No disclosures made on the total cash payments for acquisition of long-term assets.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to direct the Municipal Accountant to provide the lacking disclosures and comply with the disclosure requirements of IPSAS and Manual on NGAS for LGUs by adequately inserting a concise narrative on significant changes and breakdown of material balances to ensure that relevant information is available to the intended users of the Financial Statements.
observations.reason: Insufficient narrative disclosures in the Notes to Financial Statements

observations.recommendation:
In view of the foregoing, we have recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to:

a. Instruct the Municipal Engineer to include all projects implemented by the Municipal Government in the RGPPA, regardless of amount or source of funds for timely validation and verification; and

b. Direct the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Engineer to reconcile their records respective on CIP, at least quarterly, to facilitate the updated status of accomplishment of government programs, projects and activities or before effecting payment on PPAs.
observations.reason:
Incorporated in Finding No. 5 of AAR 2023.

Not Implemented

Incorporated in Finding No. 5 of AAR 2023.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to instruct Municipal Assessor to provide a photocopy of land titles of parcels of land to ascertain the legality of Municipality’s ownership over these parcels of land. observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
Direct the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Engineer to reconcile their records on CIP, at least quarterly, to facilitate the updated status of ongoing PPAs of the Municipality which will eventually serve as the basis for recognizing accruals in the books of accounts; and
Direct the Municipal Accountant to make necessary journal entries to recognize the accrued cost on the actual work accomplishments of the foregoing projects as of year-end. The proposed adjusting journal entries is as follows:
For the Municipal Accountant to recognize Advances to Contractors in the event of payment of a mobilization fee, rather than through the CIP account.

We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the following:
The Municipal Budget Officer and Municipal Accountant to (i) comply with the provisions of IPSAS 24 on the preparation of SCBAA; and (ii) reconcile their records so that any resulting discrepancies can be immediately verified and corrected; and
The Municipal Accountant to provide disclosures on the face of the SCBAA and/or in the Notes to FS the (i) changes between the original and final budgets; (ii) material discrepancies between the budget and actual amounts; and (iii) reconciliation of actual amounts per SCBAA and per financial statements, to enhance the transparency of the Municipality’s financial statements.

We have recommended the Municipal Mayor to instruct the Municipal Accountant to provide the lacking disclosures in the Notes to Financial Statement using the prescribed format as provided by COA Circular 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012 and comply with the disclosure requirements of IPSAS and MNGAS for LGUs. Conformity with pertinent accounting guidelines from other relevant rules and regulations should also be practiced by adequately inserting a concise narrative and detailed schedule of material balances to ensure that relevant information is available to the intended users of the Financial Statements.

We have recommended the Municipal Mayor to direct the Municipal Accountant to prepare adjusting journal entry for the total unexpended balance of LGUs share from tobacco excise tax using the proforma entry below:
Adjusting entry under General Fund if the book of the Municipality is not yet close:

Dr. Due to NGA  219,813.00

Cr. Share from Tobacco Excise Tax (RA 7171 and 8240)
219,813.00
  
Adjusting entry under General Fund if the book of the Municipality is already closed:

Dr. Due to NGA 219,813.00
Cr. Government Equity
219,813.00

We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant and the HRMO to establish control mechanisms preventing employees from availing of loans that will eventually reduce their monthly net take home pay below Five Thousand Pesos (₱5,000.00) as provided in the GAA FY 2023 so that adequate funds are available for the basic needs of each employee.

We have recommended the Municipal Mayor to instruct the Municipal Treasurer the following:
To thoroughly examine the replenishment of the PCF and to avoid granting cash advances to the PCF Custodian in excess of the required amount for replenishment, which could lead to the accumulation of a significant balance of PCF in their possession; and
To carefully manage its resources especially the PCF since this can be a potent source of mishandling and abuse. Strict compliance over PCF policy and COA Circular 97-002.

We have recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to direct the Municipal Treasurer to deposit all collections regularly and intact within the prescribed period to the authorized government depository bank as required in Section 69 of P.D 1445 and other existing regulations to avoid possible theft/ loss or misuse.

We have recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to direct the GAD Focal Person to carefully identify and assess PPAs utilizing the methodologies outlined in the PWC-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC 2016-01 to ensure that gender-related issues and concerns are effectively addressed and that procedures are done accordingly for proper implementation and monitoring of LGU GAD Plan and Budget.

We have reiterated prior year’s audit recommendations that the Municipal Mayor instruct the following:
The BAC to deliberate properly on the appropriate mode of procurement to be used, before the Municipality acquires the needed goods, thus avoiding a similar scenario to happen; and
To stop the practice of splitting purchase requisitions or contracts and other related transactions pursuant to COA Circular No. 76-41, to promote effective competition and transparency in the procurement process and obtain a fair and equitable contract for the best interest of the government.

We have recommended the Municipal Mayor through its Local Sanggunian and other officials concerned to expedite the drafting of the amended Municipal Local Revenue Code subject to approval by the SP of the Province of Pangasinan to cope with the dynamic and everchanging economic atmosphere and business environment.

We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor enjoin the Municipal Engineer to consider the application of geo-tagging of projects due to its effectiveness in providing a more transparent, accurate and reliable report of project accomplishments and further as a deterrent to possible duplication and overlapping of infrastructure projects.

We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the MPDC to coordinate with the concerned departments, such as the Municipal Social Welfare Department, Office of the Municipal Agriculturist, Municipal Health Officer, members of the SB, among others, to aid in the database formulation for expeditious approval of the plans/programs necessary in the drafting of an updated CLUP.
We also have recommended that the MPDC convene with the Local Development Council to identify the bottlenecks that might have contributed to the delay of the approval of the CLUP and to provide viable solutions in resolving any identified constraints.

We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor through its MDRRMO finalize the formulation of the PSCP to strengthen the risk management capabilities of the Municipal Government and enable it to manage its vulnerabilities in the event of disruptive events then submit the same to the Municipal Mayor for approval, and to the SB for the passage of a resolution to institutionalize the PSCP.
We further recommended that LDRRM Officer, in the event the PSCP has been approved, adopt a monitoring of operational requirements like the submission of PSCP and continuous update of the plan depending on the availability of other useful information.

We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Engineer the following:
For similar future projects, conduct effective and efficient planning through project site inspection by technical personnel to avoid delay and adjustments in the plan and implementation of project; and
Regular monitoring in the implementation of the ongoing and succeeding projects to ensure completion thereof within the target completion dates to achieve utmost benefits for the public.
In view of the foregoing, we recommend that the management provide the Audit Team of copies of approved time extensions, suspensions and variation orders or other related documents that will prove that the projects are not delayed, otherwise impose liquidated damages against the claims of the contractors who have incurred delay in the implementation of infrastructure projects or apply any other action as prescribe in RA 9184.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended the following to the Local Chief Executive:

Limit the procurement of goods and services on a reimbursement basis, unless the circumstances warrant its application ;

Strictly follow the rules and procedures of procurements provided in the Revised IRR of RA 9184; and

Require the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to ensure that payments are made by check directly to the creditors to whom payments are due and withhold the corresponding tax.
We have recommended that Management to stop the incurrence of expenses that are personal and private in nature to avoid suspension or disallowance in audit.

We have recommended for the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Budget Officer to make necessary adjusting entries to correct the charging of the foregoing expenditures. Furthermore, the Management is advised to stop the practice of charging expenditures against the Due to LGUs – 25% Barangay Share.  Instead for the LGU to release their RPT Shares directly to the concerned barangay as prescribed in Section 271 of the Local Government Code of 1991 (Republic Act No. 7160), in order to prevent the Suspension and/ or Disallowance of the same  in audit.

We have recommended that the Municipal Health Officer or the Requisitioning Officers for the procurement of drugs and medicines be required to certify as to the conformity of procured medicines with the essential drug list as contained in the Philippine National Drug Formulary, to ensure delivery of effective medicines to the general public.
observations.reason: Post audit of disbursements revealed that the only two (2) transactions do not have certification from the Municipal Health Officer/ Requisitioning Officer, as reiterated in Finding No. 15.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor stop granting/ issuing fuel and oil slip to private vehicles, instead, allow them to claim reimbursement for the equivalent fare for official travels when private vehicles is used.

We have recommended that Municipal Budget Officer and the Municipal Accountant make the necessary adjustments in the books for the proper charging of the foregoing expenditures. Also for those concern to strictly comply with policies and guidelines as regards the proper charging of expenditures against the corresponding funds so as to avoid inconveniences such as the suspensions/disallowances of the same. It is imperative that Management should only use/ disburse the 20% Development Fund only for the purpose for which the fund was appropriated.
observations.reason: Verification of the utilization of 20% Development Fund disclosed that two (2) of its projects were spent that is not in it intended purposes, as reiterated in Finding No. 19.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the HRMO to prepare payroll accurately based on the actual services rendered by an employee supported by DTR. Likewise, the Municipal Accountant to review the entries in the payroll and see to it that the amount of cash advance for payroll is not based on estimates but on actual amounts supported by DTR of the employees.

We have recommended that the payrolls of regular personnel be prepared and processed on a monthly basis. These payrolls shall be in two (2) sets, reflecting both the 1st and 2nd quicena of the month. The net pay be arrived at by adding the basic salary and all allowances less total deductions divided by two (2).

We have recommended that the Local School Board (LSB) strictly comply with the provisions of Republic Act No. 7160 and the DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Circulars in the preparation of SEF Annual Budget. It is imperative that the LSB be prudent in the utilization of the SEF so as not to jeopardized the very purpose for which it was created/ allocated.

It is further recommended and worthy of its emphasis that issues or conflicts arising from its implementation and cases not covered by the provisions be submitted to the DepEd for resolution and in consultation with the municipal government. Likewise, the municipal government to stop the practice of charging non-related expenses to SEF which are considered irregular expenditure, therefore subject to disallowance.

We have recommended that the transfer of funds to Barangays covering the implementation of certain projects of the Municipality of Umingan be covered by a Memorandum of Agreement (MOA) and other documentary requirements enumerated under Section 3.1.1 and 3.1.3 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 to provide basis for monitoring the proper use of funds. Also, transfer of funds be recorded as Due from LGUs so that liquidation from the recipient Baranagay will be required and any unutilized balance will be refunded.

We have recommended that the Municipal Accountant record the receipt of the seven percent share from the 30 percent Lotto Charity Fund from PCSO as Other General Income - Share from PCSO account and be made available for appropriation for the maintenance of RHU, and for other health, social welfare, and charitable work.

Furthermore, Notices of Delinquencies shall state therein that their real properties may be distrained to effect payment. Civil remedies be applied, if warranted by circumstances. Lastly, since the barangays are receiving their shares from the realty tax collection of the municipality, their assistance may be sought in the implementation of the municipality’s tax campaign program, particularly in the dissemination of notices of delinquency to the delinquent real property owners within their territorial jurisdiction.

In case there is really a need for consultants (i) engage only experts in their respective fields ; (ii) observe rules and regulations on the hiring of consultants as provided by COA Circular 2012-003 dated October 29, 2012 and DBM National Budget Circular No. 433 dated March 1, 1994, and            (iii) support payments for consultancy service with adequate documentation, otherwise a Notice of Suspension/ Disallowance will be served ; and
Prepare contract for the consultants to properly match that of the needs of the LCE or any officials needing assistance thereof to eliminate the dubious nature of hiring said consultants.

Recommendation:
We have recommended that Management strictly adhere to the above-cited laws, rules and regulations and specifically undertake the following:
The Municipal Treasurer and Accountant to immediately submit the foregoing missing disbursement vouchers together with complete supporting documents to avoid being suspended or disallowed in audit and to prevent the concerned personnel from inconveniences such as being confronted with the corresponding sanctions a provided by regulations and laws ;
The Municipal Treasurer to institute control in the releasing of processed DVs to concerned payees or to authorized persons only for payment of LGU’s obligations and ensure that paid disbursement vouchers are kept and secured intact as listed in the Report of Checks Issued (RCI) upon transmittal to the office of the Accountant ; and
The Municipal Accountant to transmit complete submission of accounts to facilitate thorough audit and verification of the agency’s financial transactions.

Recommendation:
We have recommended that the Municipal Health Officer or the Requisitioning Officers for the procurement of drugs and medicines be required to certify as to the conformity of procured medicines with the essential drug list as contained in the Philippine National Drug Formulary, to ensure delivery of effective medicines to the general public.

Recommendation:
We have recommended that Management:
Require the Municipal Accountant to verify and ascertain that payments for gasoline, oil and lubricants are duly supported with approved and completely filled-up Driver’s Trip Tickets for proper evaluation on the propriety and reasonableness of fuel consumption before payment is effected, to avoid suspension/ disallowance in audit ;
Direct the vehicle drivers concerned to completely fill-up or indicate the following data on the Driver’s Trip Tickets: Time of departure and arrival of the trip, balance in tank at the beginning and end of the trip, distance traveled, odometer reading, if available, liters of gasoline consumed for the trip and purpose of travel for control and monitoring; and
Direct or assign an employee responsible to prepare Monthly Report of Official Travels and the Monthly Report of Fuel Consumption for proper analysis and verification on fuel consumption.

Recommendation:
We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor stop granting/ issuing fuel and oil slip to private vehicles, instead, allow them to claim reimbursement for the equivalent fare for official travels when private vehicles is used.

Recommendation:
We have recommended the Municipal Officials to economically, efficiently and effectively utilize the resources of the Municipality by discouraging and preventing unnecessary expenditures to avoid suspension or disallowance in audit. Furthermore, to purchase essential items needed in the day to day operations of their respective Municipal Offices to prevent wastage of government funds.

Recommendation:
We have recommended that Municipal Budget Officer and the Municipal Accountant make the necessary adjustments in the books for the proper charging of the foregoing expenditures. Also for those concern to strictly comply with policies and guidelines as regards the proper charging of expenditures against the corresponding funds so as to avoid inconveniences such as the suspensions/disallowances of the same. It is imperative that Management should only use/ disburse the 20% Development Fund only for the purpose for which the fund was appropriated.

Recommendation:
We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive (a) require a written explanation from the Municipal Treasurer for the noted delays in the deposit and the retention of collections within unreasonable period; and (b) direct the Municipal Treasurer to deposit all collections regularly and intact within the prescribed period to the authorized government depository bank as required in Section 69 of P.D 1445 and other existing regulations to avoid possible theft/ loss or misuse. If warranted, reprimand the concern official.

Recommendation:
We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the HRMO to prepare payroll accurately based on the actual services rendered by an employee supported by DTR. Likewise, the Municipal Accountant to review the entries in the payroll and see to it that the amount of cash advance for payroll is not based on estimates but on actual amounts supported by DTR of the employees.

Recommendation:
We have recommended that the payrolls of regular personnel be prepared and processed on a monthly basis. These payrolls shall be in two (2) sets, reflecting both the 1st and 2nd quicena of the month. The net pay be arrived at by adding the basic salary and all allowances less total deductions divided by two (2).

Recommendation:
We have recommended that the Local School Board (LSB) strictly comply with the provisions of Republic Act No. 7160 and the DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Circulars in the preparation of SEF Annual Budget. It is imperative that the LSB be prudent in the utilization of the SEF so as not to jeopardized the very purpose for which it was created/ allocated.
It is further recommended and worthy of its emphasis that issues or conflicts arising from its implementation and cases not covered by the provisions be submitted to the DepEd for resolution and in consultation with the municipal government. Likewise, the municipal government to stop the practice of charging non-related expenses to SEF which are considered irregular expenditure, therefore subject to disallowance.

Recommendation:
We have recommended that the transfer of funds to Barangays covering the implementation of certain projects of the Municipality of Umingan be covered by a Memorandum of Agreement (MOA) and other documentary requirements enumerated under Section 3.1.1 and 3.1.3 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 to provide basis for monitoring the proper use of funds. Also, transfer of funds be recorded as Due from LGUs so that liquidation from the recipient Baranagay will be required and any unutilized balance will be refunded.

Recommendation:
We have recommended that the Municipal Treasurer facilitate the updating of the RPTARs to be able to prepare the Certified List of Taxpayers and taxes due as basis of the Municipal Accountant in setting up of RPT/ SET Receivables at the beginning of each year.

Recommendation:
We have recommended that the Municipal Accountant record the receipt of the seven percent share from the 30 percent Lotto Charity Fund from PCSO as Other General Income - Share from PCSO account and be made available for appropriation for the maintenance of RHU, and for other health, social welfare, and charitable work.

Recommendation:
We have recommended that the Municipal Accountant to record all purchases of drugs and medicine regardless of whether or not they are consumed within the accounting period as inventory following the perpetual inventory method and an inventory control account should be maintained for each inventory item pursuant to Section 114 of the Manual on NGAS, Volume I.
Further, the Municipal General Services Officer shall maintain stock cards and consolidate weekly the Requisition and Issue Slip for which supplies and materials were issued using the Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued (SSMI). The SSMI with the original copy of the RIS be submitted to the Municipal Accountant, who shall compute the cost of supplies issued and ending inventory using the prescribed measurement in PPSAS No. 12 and prepare the JEV to record the expenditures using the appropriate expense accounts.

Recommendation:
We have recommended that Management:
Municipal Accountant prepare the necessary adjusting entry to effect the share 15% and 5% share of the OBO and DPWH, respectively from the collection of building permit;
Municipal Treasurer strictly monitor the amount to be automatically retained with the City to be used for OBO’s programs and projects and the amount to be remitted to BTr representing the 5% share of DPWH; and
Municipal Engineering Office as the OBO to prepare Program of Work detailing the manner on the utilization of the 15% share from the collection of building permits, and properly observe the guideline in the preparation of the program of works.

Recommendation:
We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the GAD Focal Person to:
carefully identify and include only PPAs that are gender responsive in the GAD Plan and Budget to address the issues and concerns arising from the unequal status of women and men stakeholders, clients and constituents;
include in the annual budget the allocation for GAD Fund for those PPAs which are gender responsive and refrain from allocating for PPAs which are not GAD related; and
monitor the implementation of the GAD PPAs

Recommendation:
We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the GAD Focal Person to submit a copy of the DILG approved annual GAD Plan and Budget (GPB) and the corresponding GAD Accomplishment Report within the period prescribed under COA Circular No. 2014-001.

Recommendation:
We have recommended that Management adopt strategies to improve the collection efficiency and must exert extra effort to have efficient and aggressive tax collection. Furthermore, Notices of Delinquencies shall state therein that their real properties may be distrained to effect payment. Civil remedies be applied, if warranted by circumstances. Lastly, since the barangays are receiving their shares from the realty tax collection of the municipality, their assistance may be sought in the implementation of the municipality’s tax campaign program, particularly in the dissemination of notices of delinquency to the delinquent real property owners within their territorial jurisdiction.

Recommendation:
We have recommended that the Municipal Accountant, Treasurer and the Personnel Officer establish control mechanisms not to allow municipal employees avail loans that will eventually reduce their monthly net take home pay below Five Thousand Pesos (P 5,000.00) as provided in the General Appropriations Act- FY 2018.
We have recommended that the Municipality continue to comply with the existing laws, rules and regulations on withholding and remittance of taxes.

We have recommended that Municipal Mayor thru the Municipal General Services Office to immediately create an Inventory Committee to conduct physical count of property, plant and equipment, thereby producing the Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment.
